questions.question: What is the 'Social Cure' framework, and what makes it important for understanding collective activity and mental health?

options.text: Social Cure is a medical treatment that requires professional supervision
Social Cure is the framework showing that meaningful group membership produces mental health benefits through belonging, identity, support, and purpose
Social Cure only applies to competitive sports teams, not recreational activities
Social Cure is a temporary effect that disappears after a few weeks of group participation

questions.explanation: The Social Cure framework (developed by Haslam, Jetten, Cruwys, and colleagues) shows that belonging to meaningful social groups is a fundamental determinant of health and wellbeing. It works through four pathways: belonging (you have a place where you fit in), identity (shared sense of 'who we are'), support (people notice and encourage you), and purpose (contributing to something larger). This is one of the most well-supported theoretical frameworks in social and health psychology.

questions.question: According to the research presented, how does team sport compare to individual sport for adult mental health outcomes?

options.text: Individual sport and team sport show identical mental health benefits
Individual sport is better because it allows more personal control
Adults in team sport had more favorable mental health outcomes than individual sport participants, and this benefit exists regardless of physical activity volume
Team sport only benefits youth and adolescents, not adults

questions.explanation: The 2023 systematic review of 29 studies (adults 18-84) found that adults in team sport had MORE favorable mental health outcomes than individual sport participants across multiple domains — depression, anxiety, stress, self-esteem, life satisfaction, and sense of belonging. Critically, this benefit exists *regardless of physical activity volume,* meaning the social context (not just exercise dose) is an active ingredient in mental health benefit.

questions.question: What is the critical distinction between recreational sport and competitive/elite sport for mental health?

options.text: There is no meaningful difference — all team sport improves mental health
Recreational sport shows mental health benefits; competitive/elite sport shows mental health RISKS (increased disorders, loneliness, maltreatment exposure)
Competitive sport is always better because it builds more resilience through challenge
The distinction only matters for children, not adults

questions.explanation: This is a safety-critical distinction. Recreational and community sport (where the focus is on enjoyment, connection, and personal growth) show mental health benefits. Competitive and elite sport contexts (where performance pressure, selection, and advancement dominate) show a DIFFERENT pattern: youth elite athletes show 16.9% current mental disorders, over half of U.S. college athletes report being 'very lonely' despite team membership, and 70-80% of youth drop out by age 15 due to psychological factors (stress, reduced enjoyment). The mental health benefits of collective activity are CONDITIONAL on psychologically safe and enjoyable environments.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the five factors that make a group psychologically beneficial for mental health?

options.text: Meaningful identity formation (you identify with the group, not just participate)
High performance standards and competitive rankings to drive motivation
Psychological safety and inclusion (no judgment, exclusion, or criticism)
Regular, consistent participation over time (not one-off experiences)

questions.explanation: High performance standards and competitive rankings are NOT factors that make groups psychologically beneficial — in fact, they're risk factors for toxic team culture. The five factors that DO make groups work for mental health are: (1) meaningful identity formation, (2) psychological safety and inclusion, (3) shared goals or values, (4) regular/consistent participation, and (5) enjoyment. Enjoyment is non-negotiable: if you don't enjoy the activity, you won't sustain participation, and the mental health benefits won't materialize.

questions.question: When is collective activity or team sport NOT sufficient as a standalone approach for mental health?

options.text: Collective activity is always sufficient — you never need professional help if you're active in a group
When you experience severe depression, suicidality, or clinical-level mental health symptoms requiring professional assessment and treatment
Collective activity is never helpful for mental health — only therapy works
Team sport is only insufficient if you're not playing competitively enough

questions.explanation: This course provides evidence-based psychoeducation about the mental health benefits of collective activity — it is NOT a substitute for professional mental health treatment. If you experience severe depression, suicidality, acute mental health crises, or clinical-level symptoms, you need professional assessment and treatment. Collective activity can be a valuable COMPLEMENT to professional care — but it's not a replacement. Think of it as part of a comprehensive approach to mental health, not a standalone treatment for clinical conditions.

title: Why Collective Activity — The Social Cure - Quiz
